data.short_description: Save the Cat beat sheet for the Novel Studio app: Blake Snyder's 15-beat structure as an adoptable template. The beats live in this entity's lore; a novel adopts the sheet in its Blueprint, and its scenes' beat property uses these beat names exactly.

lore.title: Beats
lore.content:
The 15 beats of Blake Snyder's Save the Cat, in story order. Positions are percentages of the whole book — targets, not laws. Scenes' beat property must use these names exactly as spelled.

Opening Image (0–1%) — a snapshot of the protagonist's world before anything changes; the "before" photo the Final Image will answer.

Theme Stated (~5%) — someone states, usually in passing and usually to a protagonist who isn't listening, what the story will prove.

Set-Up (1–10%) — the protagonist's status quo: their world, wants, and flaws, and every character who matters later. What's broken here is what the story will fix or break further.

Catalyst (~10%) — the event that knocks the status quo over. It happens TO the protagonist; they don't choose it.

Debate (10–20%) — the protagonist resists the call: can I really do this, should I? The section asks a question the Break into Two answers.

Break into Two (~20%) — the protagonist chooses to act, stepping into the new world or plan. Act 2 begins; from here the choice is theirs.

B Story (~22%) — a new relationship (love interest, mentor, friend) that will carry the theme while the A story carries the plot.

Fun and Games (20–50%) — the promise of the premise delivered: the poster scenes, the reason the reader picked the book up. Rising success or comic failure.

Midpoint (~50%) — a false victory or false defeat that raises the stakes and ends the fun; plot and theme cross. Time clocks often start here.

Bad Guys Close In (50–75%) — external opposition tightens while internal flaws crack the team or plan from inside.

All Is Lost (~75%) — the bottom: the plan is dead, and something or someone is often lost for good (the "whiff of death").

Dark Night of the Soul (75–80%) — the protagonist sits in the wreckage and, at last, hears the theme from the inside.

Break into Three (~80%) — the A and B stories hand the protagonist the answer; armed with the lesson, they choose one more try. Act 3 begins.

Finale (80–99%) — the lesson is applied: the protagonist executes the new plan, faces the opposition, and proves the change is real.

Final Image (99–100%) — the "after" photo: the Opening Image transformed, showing without argument how much has changed.
